summary: O'Neil reviews 'Prime Numbers and the Riemann Hypothesis' by Barry Mazur and William Stein, praising it as an accessible yet rigorous introduction to deep mathematical concepts. The book combines elegant theoretical explanations with computational illustrations, covering topics from basic prime number theory to the Riemann zeta function. She particularly admires that the authors break the 'third rail' taboo of asking general audiences to engage with formulas and technical work. However, she critiques the book's heavy focus on white male mathematicians, noting the stereotype threat this poses for diverse readers.
key_points: The book successfully makes advanced mathematics accessible to readers who may have forgotten basic definitions, while still teaching new things to professional mathematicians
The author pairing works well: Barry Mazur provides elegant 'pure thought' explanations while William Stein contributes illuminating computational graphs
Unlike most popular math books, this one dares to include formulas and asks readers to work hard for understanding
The representation of mathematicians in the book skews entirely toward white males, creating potential stereotype threat for other readers
money_quotes: I learned a lot reading it, even though I'm supposed to know these things already. But I really didn't, and now I'm glad I do.

The book does exactly what most general audience books dare not do – including mine – which is to say, they use formulas, and graphs, and generally speaking ask the reader to work hard, in the name of enlightenment, beauty, and wonder.
Technical work for the reader such as this is, normally speaking, something of a third rail.
In this book the authors run straight for that rail, set up a game of catch, and invite you to join their picnic in the rain, electric shock be damned.
They're having so much fun that you can't help yourself.
As an educator I want to be aware of the stereotype threat that this sets up for other readers.
key_insight: The best popular mathematics writing dares to ask readers to engage with real formulas and technical concepts rather than dumbing things down, but must also consider whose stories get told.
